name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data loading, preprocessing, and velocity field characterization
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets (`vortex_filament_tracers.npy` and `levy_walk_3d_trajectories.npy`) from their exact provided paths
        - For each tracer in the vortex filament dataset, compute instantaneous velocity vectors (vx, vy, vz) using central differences on the noise-free (x_true, y_true, z_true) coordinates; truncate first and last time steps
        - Compute velocity variance σ_v²(N) for each filament configuration N ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}
        - Compute speed distributions |v| for each N configuration
        - For the Lévy walk dataset, compute velocities similarly for each β class
        - Save all computed velocity arrays, variance statistics, and trajectory metadata to disk as numpy/npz files for use in subsequent steps
        - Print detailed summary statistics: mean speed, velocity variance, number of tracers per N, time range, dt

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: 3D MSD scaling and anomalous exponent estimation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load preprocessed velocity and trajectory data from Step 1
        - For each N configuration, compute the Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer using the 3D displacement (x²+y²+z²) from the noise-free coordinates
        - Ensemble-average TAMSD over tracers for each N to obtain ⟨MSD(Δt)⟩
        - Compute the local log-log slope (derivative) of ⟨MSD(Δt)⟩ vs Δt to identify the stable scaling region
        - Fit power-law MSD ~ Δt^α in the identified stable window (avoiding ballistic onset and saturation) for each N; report α(N) with uncertainty
        - Repeat the same TAMSD computation and power-law fitting for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
        - Save all MSD arrays, local slopes, and fitted α values to disk
        - Print α(N) for all vortex configurations and α for all Lévy walk β classes with confidence intervals

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: VACF analysis and residence time distributions
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity arrays from Step 1
        - Compute the normalized 3D VACF C_v(Δt) = ⟨v(t)·v(t+Δt)⟩/⟨|v(t)|²⟩ for each N configuration of the vortex filament system, ensemble-averaged over all tracers
        - Identify the zero-crossing time τ_c for each N
        - Compute the VACF for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
        - For residence time analysis: define speed threshold at 25th percentile of speed (slow/trapped events) for each N; identify contiguous time intervals where speed is below threshold; record durations of these intervals
        - Fit power-law P(τ) ~ τ^{-(1+β_trap)} to the residence time distributions using log-log regression or MLE
        - Save VACF arrays, zero-crossing times, residence time arrays, and fitted β_trap values to disk
        - Print zero-crossing times and β_trap estimates for each N

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Velocity PDF tail analysis and Holtsmark distribution testing
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity arrays from Step 1
        - For each N configuration, construct the empirical marginal PDF of velocity components (vx) and the speed distribution P(|v|)
        - Perform tail analysis using Hill estimator on the upper tail of the speed distribution to estimate the stability index α_stable for each N
        - Fit a power-law to the tail of P(|v|) using log-log regression on the CCDF (complementary CDF) to estimate the tail exponent; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark value of -5/2 (i.e., P(v) ~ v^{-5/2})
        - Perform KS test comparing empirical velocity distributions against a theoretical Lévy-stable distribution with α_stable=1.5 (Holtsmark) for each N
        - Compute displacement PDFs: for each N, collect all Δx displacements at multiple lag times (e.g., Δt = 1, 5, 10 steps); fit Gaussian and Lévy-stable distributions to these marginal PDFs
        - Save all PDF arrays, Hill estimates, tail exponents, KS statistics, and displacement PDF data to disk
        - Print Hill estimates of α_stable and KS test results for each N

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Ergodicity breaking parameter calculation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load TAMSD arrays from Step 2
        - For each N configuration, compute the ergodicity breaking (EB) parameter: EB = (⟨TAMSD²⟩ - ⟨TAMSD⟩²) / ⟨TAMSD⟩² at a fixed lag time (use Δt = T/4 where T is total trajectory length)
        - Compute EB as a function of lag time Δt to show its dependence
        - Repeat EB calculation for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
        - Perform bootstrapping (at least 500 resamples) on tracer trajectories to generate confidence intervals for α(N) from Step 2 and for EB(N)
        - Save EB values, EB vs Δt arrays, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk
        - Print EB(N) for all vortex configurations and EB for all Lévy walk β classes

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: Generate all analysis plots
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
        - **Plot 1**: Multi-panel MSD scaling figure — log-log MSD vs Δt for all N configurations (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) with fitted power-law lines and α annotations; include local slope panels
        - **Plot 2**: VACF figure — normalized VACF vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) in separate panels; mark zero-crossing times with vertical dashed lines
        - **Plot 3**: Velocity PDF and tail analysis — log-log plot of P(|v|) for each N with theoretical Holtsmark slope (-5/2) reference line; include marginal vx PDF with Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits
        - **Plot 4**: Displacement PDF evolution — marginal Δx PDF at multiple lag times for N=40 vortex system; overlay Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits; show non-Gaussianity
        - **Plot 5**: Residence time distributions — log-log P(τ) for each N with fitted power-law lines and β_trap annotations
        - **Plot 6**: Ergodicity breaking — EB parameter vs N for vortex system and vs β for Lévy walk; include EB vs Δt curves
        - **Plot 7**: Regime map — (EB, α) plane with vortex filament points (colored by N), Lévy walk points (colored by β), and theoretical predictions marked; include reference lines for normal diffusion (α=1, EB=0)
        - Save all plots as high-resolution PNG files

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Statistical validation via bootstrapping and KS tests
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
        - Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples) on tracer ensembles for each N to generate 95% confidence intervals for α(N) and EB(N)
        - Conduct KS tests comparing empirical speed distributions against theoretical Holtsmark (Lévy-stable with α_stable=1.5) for each N
        - Conduct KS tests comparing empirical displacement PDFs (at multiple lag times) against Gaussian null hypothesis for each N
        - Print all KS statistics, p-values, and bootstrap confidence intervals in a detailed summary table
        - Save validation results to disk

- Step 8:
      * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a comprehensive Results section
        - Report velocity variance σ_v²(N) and its scaling with N; discuss implications for disorder intensity
        - Report α(N) for all vortex configurations with confidence intervals; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark prediction α=1.5 and the 2D Cauchy prediction α→1; discuss convergence with increasing N
        - Report VACF zero-crossing times τ_c(N) and compare against Lévy walk VACF profiles; discuss the role of quenched spatial disorder vs stochastic renewal in generating velocity memory
        - Report Hill estimates of α_stable and KS test results for the velocity PDF tail analysis; assess convergence toward the Holtsmark distribution
        - Report displacement PDF non-Gaussianity and its evolution with lag time; discuss the transition from Lévy-stable to Gaussian behavior (if any)
        - Report residence time tail exponents β_trap(N) and their connection to the anomalous diffusion exponent
        - Report EB(N) values and their dependence on N; compare against Lévy walk EB values; discuss ergodicity breaking as a function of filament density
        - Report regime map positions of all systems in the (EB, α) plane; explicitly contrast 3D vortex filament results with 2D point-vortex results
        - Reference all generated plots by their descriptive names
        - Provide a coherent physical interpretation connecting all results to the Holtsmark distribution theory and the 3D Biot-Savart kernel

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: Data loading, preprocessing, and velocity field characterization
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load both datasets (vortex_filament_tracers.npy and levy_walk_3d_trajectories.npy) from their exact provided paths
		- For each tracer in the vortex filament dataset, compute instantaneous velocity vectors (vx, vy, vz) using central differences on the noise-free (x_true, y_true, z_true) coordinates; truncate first and last time steps
		- Compute velocity variance σ_v²(N) for each filament configuration N ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}
		- Compute speed distributions |v| for each N configuration
		- For the Lévy walk dataset, compute velocities similarly for each β class
		- Save all computed velocity arrays, variance statistics, and trajectory metadata to disk as numpy/npz files for use in subsequent steps
		- Print detailed summary statistics: mean speed, velocity variance, number of tracers per N, time range, dt

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: 3D MSD scaling and anomalous exponent estimation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load preprocessed velocity and trajectory data from Step 1
		- For each N configuration, compute the Time-Averaged MSD (TAMSD) for each individual tracer using the 3D displacement (x²+y²+z²) from the noise-free coordinates
		- Ensemble-average TAMSD over tracers for each N to obtain ⟨MSD(Δt)⟩
		- Compute the local log-log slope (derivative) of ⟨MSD(Δt)⟩ vs Δt to identify the stable scaling region
		- Fit power-law MSD ~ Δt^α in the identified stable window (avoiding ballistic onset and saturation) for each N; report α(N) with uncertainty
		- Repeat the same TAMSD computation and power-law fitting for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
		- Save all MSD arrays, local slopes, and fitted α values to disk
		- Print α(N) for all vortex configurations and α for all Lévy walk β classes with confidence intervals

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: VACF analysis and residence time distributions
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity arrays from Step 1
		- Compute the normalized 3D VACF C_v(Δt) = ⟨v(t)·v(t+Δt)⟩/⟨|v(t)|²⟩ for each N configuration of the vortex filament system, ensemble-averaged over all tracers
		- Identify the zero-crossing time τ_c for each N
		- Compute the VACF for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
		- For residence time analysis: define speed threshold at 25th percentile of speed (slow/trapped events) for each N; identify contiguous time intervals where speed is below threshold; record durations of these intervals
		- Fit power-law P(τ) ~ τ^{-(1+β_trap)} to the residence time distributions using log-log regression or MLE
		- Save VACF arrays, zero-crossing times, residence time arrays, and fitted β_trap values to disk
		- Print zero-crossing times and β_trap estimates for each N

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Velocity PDF tail analysis and Holtsmark distribution testing
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity arrays from Step 1
		- For each N configuration, construct the empirical marginal PDF of velocity components (vx) and the speed distribution P(|v|)
		- Perform tail analysis using Hill estimator on the upper tail of the speed distribution to estimate the stability index α_stable for each N
		- Fit a power-law to the tail of P(|v|) using log-log regression on the CCDF (complementary CDF) to estimate the tail exponent; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark value of -5/2 (i.e., P(v) ~ v^{-5/2})
		- Perform KS test comparing empirical velocity distributions against a theoretical Lévy-stable distribution with α_stable=1.5 (Holtsmark) for each N
		- Compute displacement PDFs: for each N, collect all Δx displacements at multiple lag times (e.g., Δt = 1, 5, 10 steps); fit Gaussian and Lévy-stable distributions to these marginal PDFs
		- Save all PDF arrays, Hill estimates, tail exponents, KS statistics, and displacement PDF data to disk
		- Print Hill estimates of α_stable and KS test results for each N

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Ergodicity breaking parameter calculation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load TAMSD arrays from Step 2
		- For each N configuration, compute the ergodicity breaking (EB) parameter: EB = (⟨TAMSD²⟩ - ⟨TAMSD⟩²) / ⟨TAMSD⟩² at a fixed lag time (use Δt = T/4 where T is total trajectory length)
		- Compute EB as a function of lag time Δt to show its dependence
		- Repeat EB calculation for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
		- Perform bootstrapping (at least 500 resamples) on tracer trajectories to generate confidence intervals for α(N) from Step 2 and for EB(N)
		- Save EB values, EB vs Δt arrays, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk
		- Print EB(N) for all vortex configurations and EB for all Lévy walk β classes

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: Generate all analysis plots
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
		- Plot 1: Multi-panel MSD scaling figure — log-log MSD vs Δt for all N configurations (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) with fitted power-law lines and α annotations; include local slope panels
		- Plot 2: VACF figure — normalized VACF vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) in separate panels; mark zero-crossing times with vertical dashed lines
		- Plot 3: Velocity PDF and tail analysis — log-log plot of P(|v|) for each N with theoretical Holtsmark slope (-5/2) reference line; include marginal vx PDF with Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits
		- Plot 4: Displacement PDF evolution — marginal Δx PDF at multiple lag times for N=40 vortex system; overlay Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits; show non-Gaussianity
		- Plot 5: Residence time distributions — log-log P(τ) for each N with fitted power-law lines and β_trap annotations
		- Plot 6: Ergodicity breaking — EB parameter vs N for vortex system and vs β for Lévy walk; include EB vs Δt curves
		- Plot 7: Regime map — (EB, α) plane with vortex filament points (colored by N), Lévy walk points (colored by β), and theoretical predictions marked; include reference lines for normal diffusion (α=1, EB=0)
		- Save all plots as high-resolution PNG files

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Statistical validation via bootstrapping and KS tests
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
		- Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples) on tracer ensembles for each N to generate 95% confidence intervals for α(N) and EB(N)
		- Conduct KS tests comparing empirical speed distributions against theoretical Holtsmark (Lévy-stable with α_stable=1.5) for each N
		- Conduct KS tests comparing empirical displacement PDFs (at multiple lag times) against Gaussian null hypothesis for each N
		- Print all KS statistics, p-values, and bootstrap confidence intervals in a detailed summary table
		- Save validation results to disk

- Step 8:
	* sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a comprehensive Results section
		- Report velocity variance σ_v²(N) and its scaling with N; discuss implications for disorder intensity
		- Report α(N) for all vortex configurations with confidence intervals; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark prediction α=1.5 and the 2D Cauchy prediction α→1; discuss convergence with increasing N
		- Report VACF zero-crossing times τ_c(N) and compare against Lévy walk VACF profiles; discuss the role of quenched spatial disorder vs stochastic renewal in generating velocity memory
		- Report Hill estimates of α_stable and KS test results for the velocity PDF tail analysis; assess convergence toward the Holtsmark distribution
		- Report displacement PDF non-Gaussianity and its evolution with lag time; discuss the transition from Lévy-stable to Gaussian behavior (if any)
		- Report residence time tail exponents β_trap(N) and their connection to the anomalous diffusion exponent
		- Report EB(N) values and their dependence on N; compare against Lévy walk EB values; discuss ergodicity breaking as a function of filament density
		- Report regime map positions of all systems in the (EB, α) plane; explicitly contrast 3D vortex filament results with 2D point-vortex results
		- Reference all generated plots by their descriptive names
		- Provide a coherent physical interpretation connecting all results to the Holtsmark distribution theory and the 3D Biot-Savart kernel

name: plan_reviewer
content:
**Recommendations:**

- In Step 1, the engineer should also explicitly verify the data integrity of both loaded files by printing the total number of rows, unique trajectory IDs, unique N values, unique β values, and the dtype of each field, before proceeding to any computation; this guards against silent data loading errors that would propagate through all subsequent steps.

- In Step 1, when computing central-difference velocities for the vortex filament dataset, the engineer must reshape the flat structured array into per-tracer trajectory matrices (indexed by trajectory_id and n_filaments) before applying the finite difference, since the data is stored as a flat array of 10,000 rows rather than pre-shaped trajectory blocks; failure to do this will produce incorrect velocity estimates at trajectory boundaries.

- In Step 2, the TAMSD computation should explicitly use the formula TAMSD(Δt) = (1/(T-Δt)) * sum_{t=0}^{T-Δt} [r(t+Δt) - r(t)]² rather than displacement from the origin, since the pre-computed msd_true field measures displacement from origin (EAMSD), not the time-averaged sliding-window MSD; the engineer should clearly distinguish between TAMSD and EAMSD in all saved outputs and printed results.

- In Step 2, the stable scaling window for power-law fitting should be determined programmatically from the local log-log slope plot rather than using a fixed fraction of the trajectory length; specifically, the engineer should identify the lag-time range where the local slope is approximately constant (within a tolerance of ±0.1) and use that range for fitting, printing the identified window boundaries for each N.

- In Step 3, the VACF computation must account for the fact that each tracer has a different velocity variance due to quenched disorder; the normalization ⟨|v(t)|²⟩ should be computed per-tracer and then the normalized VACF should be ensemble-averaged, rather than normalizing by the global ensemble-averaged variance, to avoid bias from high-variance outlier tracers dominating the normalization.

- In Step 3, for the residence time distribution fitting, the engineer should use maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) for the power-law exponent rather than log-log linear regression on binned histograms, since binning introduces systematic bias in tail exponent estimation; the MLE estimator for a discrete power law or continuous Pareto distribution should be applied directly to the raw residence time durations.

- In Step 4, the Hill estimator requires a choice of the number of upper-order statistics k; the engineer should compute the Hill estimate as a function of k (the Hill plot) and identify the stable plateau region to select the optimal k, rather than using a single fixed k value; the selected k and the resulting α_stable estimate should be printed for each N.

- In Step 4, when fitting Lévy-stable distributions to displacement PDFs, the engineer should use scipy.stats.levy_stable.fit with the stability index constrained to the range [1.0, 2.0] and should report the fitted stability index, scale, and location parameters separately for each lag time and each N, rather than only reporting a single summary statistic.

- In Step 5, the bootstrapping for confidence intervals on α(N) should resample at the tracer level (i.e., resample with replacement from the set of 5 tracers per N configuration), not at the time-step level, since the tracers are the independent statistical units; the engineer should print the bootstrap distribution width and confirm that 5 tracers per N is a small sample that will produce wide confidence intervals, which is physically meaningful and should be reported honestly.

- In Step 6, Plot 7 (the regime map) should include not only the 3D vortex filament and Lévy walk points but also explicitly annotate the theoretical Holtsmark prediction point (α=1.5, EB=0 for ergodic limit) and the normal diffusion point (α=1.0, EB=0), and if 2D point-vortex results from superdiffusion_v2 are available in the workspace, they should be loaded and overlaid; if not available, the plot should include a clearly labeled placeholder region indicating where 2D results would fall based on the Cauchy prediction.

- In Step 7, the KS test against the theoretical Holtsmark distribution should use the scipy.stats.levy_stable CDF with α_stable=1.5, β_skew=0 (symmetric), and scale/location parameters estimated from the empirical data (not fixed at arbitrary values), since using incorrect scale parameters will trivially reject the null hypothesis regardless of the true tail behavior; the engineer should print the fitted scale and location parameters used in each KS test.

- In Step 8, the researcher should explicitly quantify and discuss the finite-sample effect arising from only 5 tracers per N configuration, noting that this severely limits the statistical power of the EB parameter estimates and bootstrap confidence intervals, and should frame this as a limitation of the dataset rather than a methodological failure.

name: reviewer_response_evaluator
content:
**Recommendations:**

In Step 1, the engineer should also explicitly verify the data integrity of both loaded files by printing the total number of rows, unique trajectory IDs, unique N values, unique β values, and the dtype of each field, before proceeding to any computation; this guards against silent data loading errors that would propagate through all subsequent steps.

In Step 1, when computing central-difference velocities for the vortex filament dataset, the engineer must reshape the flat structured array into per-tracer trajectory matrices (indexed by trajectory_id and n_filaments) before applying the finite difference, since the data is stored as a flat array of 10,000 rows rather than pre-shaped trajectory blocks; failure to do this will produce incorrect velocity estimates at trajectory boundaries.

In Step 2, the TAMSD computation should explicitly use the formula TAMSD(Δt) = (1/(T-Δt)) * sum_{t=0}^{T-Δt} [r(t+Δt) - r(t)]² rather than displacement from the origin, since the pre-computed msd_true field measures displacement from origin (EAMSD), not the time-averaged sliding-window MSD; the engineer should clearly distinguish between TAMSD and EAMSD in all saved outputs and printed results.

In Step 2, the stable scaling window for power-law fitting should be determined programmatically from the local log-log slope plot rather than using a fixed fraction of the trajectory length; specifically, the engineer should identify the lag-time range where the local slope is approximately constant (within a tolerance of ±0.1) and use that range for fitting, printing the identified window boundaries for each N.

In Step 3, the VACF computation must account for the fact that each tracer has a different velocity variance due to quenched disorder; the normalization ⟨|v(t)|²⟩ should be computed per-tracer and then the normalized VACF should be ensemble-averaged, rather than normalizing by the global ensemble-averaged variance, to avoid bias from high-variance outlier tracers dominating the normalization.

In Step 3, for the residence time distribution fitting, the engineer should use maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) for the power-law exponent rather than log-log linear regression on binned histograms, since binning introduces systematic bias in tail exponent estimation; the MLE estimator for a discrete power law or continuous Pareto distribution should be applied directly to the raw residence time durations.

In Step 4, the Hill estimator requires a choice of the number of upper-order statistics k; the engineer should compute the Hill estimate as a function of k (the Hill plot) and identify the stable plateau region to select the optimal k, rather than using a single fixed k value; the selected k and the resulting α_stable estimate should be printed for each N.

In Step 4, when fitting Lévy-stable distributions to displacement PDFs, the engineer should use scipy.stats.levy_stable.fit with the stability index constrained to the range [1.0, 2.0] and should report the fitted stability index, scale, and location parameters separately for each lag time and each N, rather than only reporting a single summary statistic.

In Step 5, the bootstrapping for confidence intervals on α(N) should resample at the tracer level (i.e., resample with replacement from the set of 5 tracers per N configuration), not at the time-step level, since the tracers are the independent statistical units; the engineer should print the bootstrap distribution width and confirm that 5 tracers per N is a small sample that will produce wide confidence intervals, which is physically meaningful and should be reported honestly.

In Step 6, Plot 7 (the regime map) should include not only the 3D vortex filament and Lévy walk points but also explicitly annotate the theoretical Holtsmark prediction point (α=1.5, EB=0 for ergodic limit) and the normal diffusion point (α=1.0, EB=0), and if 2D point-vortex results from superdiffusion_v2 are available in the workspace, they should be loaded and overlaid; if not available, the plot should include a clearly labeled placeholder region indicating where 2D results would fall based on the Cauchy prediction.

In Step 7, the KS test against the theoretical Holtsmark distribution should use the scipy.stats.levy_stable CDF with α_stable=1.5, β_skew=0 (symmetric), and scale/location parameters estimated from the empirical data (not fixed at arbitrary values), since using incorrect scale parameters will trivially reject the null hypothesis regardless of the true tail behavior; the engineer should print the fitted scale and location parameters used in each KS test.

In Step 8, the researcher should explicitly quantify and discuss the finite-sample effect arising from only 5 tracers per N configuration, noting that this severely limits the statistical power of the EB parameter estimates and bootstrap confidence intervals, and should frame this as a limitation of the dataset rather than a methodological failure.

name: review_recorder
content:
Recommendations have been logged.
Number of feedback rounds left: 0.
Now, update the plan accordingly, planner!

name: planner
content:
**Plan:**

- Step 1:
      * sub-task: Data loading, integrity verification, preprocessing, and velocity field characterization
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load both datasets from their exact provided paths (`/home/node/work/projects/superdiffusion_v3/vortex_filament_tracers.npy` and `/home/node/work/projects/superdiffusion_v3/levy_walk_3d_trajectories.npy`)
        - Verify data integrity: print total number of rows, unique trajectory IDs, unique N values, unique β values, and dtype of each field for both datasets
        - Reshape the flat vortex filament structured array into per-tracer trajectory matrices (grouped by trajectory_id and n_filaments) before computing velocities, to avoid boundary artifacts
        - Compute instantaneous velocity vectors (vx, vy, vz) using central differences on noise-free (x_true, y_true, z_true) coordinates; truncate first and last time steps per tracer
        - Compute velocity variance σ_v²(N) and mean speed for each filament configuration N ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}
        - Compute speed distributions |v| for each N configuration
        - For the Lévy walk dataset, reshape per-trajectory and compute velocities similarly for each β class
        - Save all computed velocity arrays, variance statistics, and trajectory metadata to disk as npz files
        - Print detailed summary statistics: mean speed, velocity variance, number of tracers per N, time range, dt for both datasets

- Step 2:
      * sub-task: 3D MSD scaling and anomalous exponent estimation
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load preprocessed trajectory data from Step 1
        - For each N configuration, compute the TAMSD for each individual tracer using the sliding-window formula TAMSD(Δt) = (1/(T-Δt)) * Σ [r(t+Δt) - r(t)]² on noise-free 3D coordinates; clearly distinguish this from the EAMSD (displacement from origin)
        - Ensemble-average TAMSD over tracers for each N to obtain ⟨TAMSD(Δt)⟩
        - Compute the local log-log slope of ⟨TAMSD(Δt)⟩ vs Δt; programmatically identify the stable scaling window where the local slope is approximately constant within ±0.1 tolerance; print the identified window boundaries for each N
        - Fit power-law TAMSD ~ Δt^α within the identified stable window for each N; report α(N) with uncertainty
        - Repeat the same TAMSD computation and power-law fitting for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
        - Save all TAMSD arrays (per-tracer and ensemble-averaged), local slopes, fitted α values, and scaling windows to disk
        - Print α(N) for all vortex configurations and α for all Lévy walk β classes

- Step 3:
      * sub-task: VACF analysis and residence time distributions
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity arrays from Step 1
        - Compute the normalized 3D VACF per tracer: normalize each tracer's velocity dot-product by that tracer's own velocity variance ⟨|v(t)|²⟩, then ensemble-average the normalized VACFs across tracers for each N
        - Identify the zero-crossing time τ_c for each N from the ensemble-averaged VACF
        - Compute the VACF for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth using the same per-trajectory normalization
        - For residence time analysis: define speed threshold at 25th percentile of speed for each N; identify contiguous time intervals where speed is below threshold; record durations of these intervals
        - Fit power-law P(τ) ~ τ^{-(1+β_trap)} using MLE (Pareto MLE estimator) applied directly to raw residence time durations, not binned histograms
        - Save VACF arrays, zero-crossing times, residence time arrays, and fitted β_trap values to disk
        - Print zero-crossing times and β_trap MLE estimates for each N

- Step 4:
      * sub-task: Velocity PDF tail analysis and Holtsmark distribution testing
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load velocity arrays from Step 1
        - For each N configuration, construct the empirical marginal PDF of velocity component vx and the speed distribution P(|v|)
        - Compute the Hill estimator as a function of k (number of upper-order statistics) to produce a Hill plot; identify the stable plateau region to select optimal k; print selected k and resulting α_stable estimate for each N
        - Fit a power-law to the tail of P(|v|) using log-log regression on the CCDF; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark tail exponent of -5/2
        - Compute displacement PDFs: for each N, collect all Δx displacements at multiple lag times (Δt = 1, 5, 10 steps); fit Lévy-stable distributions using scipy.stats.levy_stable.fit with stability index constrained to [1.0, 2.0]; report fitted stability index, scale, and location parameters separately for each lag time and N
        - Save all PDF arrays, Hill estimates, tail exponents, KS statistics, and displacement PDF data to disk
        - Print Hill estimates of α_stable and tail exponent fits for each N

- Step 5:
      * sub-task: Ergodicity breaking parameter calculation and bootstrap confidence intervals
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load per-tracer TAMSD arrays from Step 2
        - For each N configuration, compute the EB parameter EB = (⟨TAMSD²⟩ - ⟨TAMSD⟩²) / ⟨TAMSD⟩² at fixed lag time Δt = T/4
        - Compute EB as a function of lag time Δt for each N
        - Repeat EB calculation for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
        - Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples) resampling at the tracer level (resample with replacement from the 5 tracers per N) to generate 95% confidence intervals for both α(N) and EB(N); print bootstrap distribution width and note that 5 tracers per N produces wide intervals
        - Save EB values, EB vs Δt arrays, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk
        - Print EB(N) for all vortex configurations and EB for all Lévy walk β classes with bootstrap confidence intervals

- Step 6:
      * sub-task: Generate all analysis plots
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
        - **Plot 1**: Multi-panel MSD scaling — log-log ⟨TAMSD⟩ vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) with fitted power-law lines and α annotations; include local slope sub-panels
        - **Plot 2**: VACF — normalized VACF vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) in separate panels; mark zero-crossing times with vertical dashed lines
        - **Plot 3**: Velocity PDF and tail analysis — log-log P(|v|) for each N with theoretical Holtsmark slope (-5/2) reference line; include marginal vx PDF with Lévy-stable fits
        - **Plot 4**: Displacement PDF evolution — marginal Δx PDF at multiple lag times for N=40 vortex system; overlay Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits to show non-Gaussianity
        - **Plot 5**: Residence time distributions — log-log P(τ) for each N with fitted power-law lines and β_trap annotations
        - **Plot 6**: Ergodicity breaking — EB parameter vs N for vortex system and vs β for Lévy walk; include EB vs Δt curves
        - **Plot 7**: Regime map — (EB, α) plane with vortex filament points (colored by N), Lévy walk points (colored by β); annotate theoretical Holtsmark prediction (α=1.5, EB=0) and normal diffusion (α=1.0, EB=0); if 2D superdiffusion_v2 results are available in the workspace load and overlay them, otherwise include a labeled placeholder region for the 2D Cauchy prediction
        - Save all plots as high-resolution PNG files

- Step 7:
      * sub-task: Statistical validation via bootstrapping and KS tests
      * agent: engineer
      * bullet points:
        - Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
        - Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples at tracer level) for each N to generate 95% confidence intervals for α(N) and EB(N); print bootstrap distribution widths
        - Conduct KS tests comparing empirical speed distributions against theoretical Holtsmark (scipy.stats.levy_stable with α_stable=1.5, β_skew=0); first estimate scale and location parameters from the empirical data and use those fitted parameters in the KS test; print fitted scale and location parameters alongside KS statistics and p-values for each N
        - Conduct KS tests comparing empirical displacement PDFs at multiple lag times against Gaussian null hypothesis for each N; print KS statistics and p-values
        - Print all results in a detailed summary table
        - Save all validation results to disk

- Step 8:
      * sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
      * agent: researcher
      * bullet points:
        - Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a comprehensive Results section
        - Report velocity variance σ_v²(N) and its scaling with N; discuss implications for disorder intensity
        - Report α(N) for all vortex configurations with bootstrap confidence intervals; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark prediction α=1.5 and the 2D Cauchy prediction α→1; discuss convergence with increasing N
        - Report VACF zero-crossing times τ_c(N) and compare against Lévy walk VACF profiles; discuss the role of quenched spatial disorder vs stochastic renewal in generating velocity memory
        - Report Hill estimates of α_stable and tail exponent fits for the velocity PDF; assess convergence toward the Holtsmark distribution
        - Report displacement PDF non-Gaussianity and its evolution with lag time; discuss the Lévy-stable fitted parameters
        - Report residence time tail exponents β_trap(N) and their connection to the anomalous diffusion exponent
        - Report EB(N) values with bootstrap confidence intervals; compare against Lévy walk EB values; discuss ergodicity breaking as a function of filament density
        - Explicitly quantify and discuss the finite-sample effect from only 5 tracers per N configuration as a dataset limitation
        - Report regime map positions of all systems in the (EB, α) plane; explicitly contrast 3D vortex filament results with 2D point-vortex results
        - Reference all generated plots by their descriptive names
        - Provide a coherent physical interpretation connecting all results to the Holtsmark distribution theory and the 3D Biot-Savart kernel

name: planner_response_evaluator
content:
**PLAN**

- Step 1:
	* sub-task: Data loading, integrity verification, preprocessing, and velocity field characterization
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load both datasets from their exact provided paths (/home/node/work/projects/superdiffusion_v3/vortex_filament_tracers.npy and /home/node/work/projects/superdiffusion_v3/levy_walk_3d_trajectories.npy)
		- Verify data integrity: print total number of rows, unique trajectory IDs, unique N values, unique β values, and dtype of each field for both datasets
		- Reshape the flat vortex filament structured array into per-tracer trajectory matrices (grouped by trajectory_id and n_filaments) before computing velocities, to avoid boundary artifacts
		- Compute instantaneous velocity vectors (vx, vy, vz) using central differences on noise-free (x_true, y_true, z_true) coordinates; truncate first and last time steps per tracer
		- Compute velocity variance σ_v²(N) and mean speed for each filament configuration N ∈ {5, 10, 20, 40}
		- Compute speed distributions |v| for each N configuration
		- For the Lévy walk dataset, reshape per-trajectory and compute velocities similarly for each β class
		- Save all computed velocity arrays, variance statistics, and trajectory metadata to disk as npz files
		- Print detailed summary statistics: mean speed, velocity variance, number of tracers per N, time range, dt for both datasets

- Step 2:
	* sub-task: 3D MSD scaling and anomalous exponent estimation
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load preprocessed trajectory data from Step 1
		- For each N configuration, compute the TAMSD for each individual tracer using the sliding-window formula TAMSD(Δt) = (1/(T-Δt)) * Σ [r(t+Δt) - r(t)]² on noise-free 3D coordinates; clearly distinguish this from the EAMSD (displacement from origin)
		- Ensemble-average TAMSD over tracers for each N to obtain ⟨TAMSD(Δt)⟩
		- Compute the local log-log slope of ⟨TAMSD(Δt)⟩ vs Δt; programmatically identify the stable scaling window where the local slope is approximately constant within ±0.1 tolerance; print the identified window boundaries for each N
		- Fit power-law TAMSD ~ Δt^α within the identified stable window for each N; report α(N) with uncertainty
		- Repeat the same TAMSD computation and power-law fitting for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
		- Save all TAMSD arrays (per-tracer and ensemble-averaged), local slopes, fitted α values, and scaling windows to disk
		- Print α(N) for all vortex configurations and α for all Lévy walk β classes

- Step 3:
	* sub-task: VACF analysis and residence time distributions
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity arrays from Step 1
		- Compute the normalized 3D VACF per tracer: normalize each tracer's velocity dot-product by that tracer's own velocity variance ⟨|v(t)|²⟩, then ensemble-average the normalized VACFs across tracers for each N
		- Identify the zero-crossing time τ_c for each N from the ensemble-averaged VACF
		- Compute the VACF for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth using the same per-trajectory normalization
		- For residence time analysis: define speed threshold at 25th percentile of speed for each N; identify contiguous time intervals where speed is below threshold; record durations of these intervals
		- Fit power-law P(τ) ~ τ^{-(1+β_trap)} using MLE (Pareto MLE estimator) applied directly to raw residence time durations, not binned histograms
		- Save VACF arrays, zero-crossing times, residence time arrays, and fitted β_trap values to disk
		- Print zero-crossing times and β_trap MLE estimates for each N

- Step 4:
	* sub-task: Velocity PDF tail analysis and Holtsmark distribution testing
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load velocity arrays from Step 1
		- For each N configuration, construct the empirical marginal PDF of velocity component vx and the speed distribution P(|v|)
		- Compute the Hill estimator as a function of k (number of upper-order statistics) to produce a Hill plot; identify the stable plateau region to select optimal k; print selected k and resulting α_stable estimate for each N
		- Fit a power-law to the tail of P(|v|) using log-log regression on the CCDF; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark tail exponent of -5/2
		- Compute displacement PDFs: for each N, collect all Δx displacements at multiple lag times (Δt = 1, 5, 10 steps); fit Lévy-stable distributions using scipy.stats.levy_stable.fit with stability index constrained to [1.0, 2.0]; report fitted stability index, scale, and location parameters separately for each lag time and N
		- Save all PDF arrays, Hill estimates, tail exponents, KS statistics, and displacement PDF data to disk
		- Print Hill estimates of α_stable and tail exponent fits for each N

- Step 5:
	* sub-task: Ergodicity breaking parameter calculation and bootstrap confidence intervals
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load per-tracer TAMSD arrays from Step 2
		- For each N configuration, compute the EB parameter EB = (⟨TAMSD²⟩ - ⟨TAMSD⟩²) / ⟨TAMSD⟩² at fixed lag time Δt = T/4
		- Compute EB as a function of lag time Δt for each N
		- Repeat EB calculation for each β class of the Lévy walk ground truth
		- Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples) resampling at the tracer level (resample with replacement from the 5 tracers per N) to generate 95% confidence intervals for both α(N) and EB(N); print bootstrap distribution width and note that 5 tracers per N produces wide intervals
		- Save EB values, EB vs Δt arrays, and bootstrap confidence intervals to disk
		- Print EB(N) for all vortex configurations and EB for all Lévy walk β classes with bootstrap confidence intervals

- Step 6:
	* sub-task: Generate all analysis plots
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
		- Plot 1: Multi-panel MSD scaling — log-log ⟨TAMSD⟩ vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) with fitted power-law lines and α annotations; include local slope sub-panels
		- Plot 2: VACF — normalized VACF vs Δt for all N (vortex) and all β classes (Lévy walk) in separate panels; mark zero-crossing times with vertical dashed lines
		- Plot 3: Velocity PDF and tail analysis — log-log P(|v|) for each N with theoretical Holtsmark slope (-5/2) reference line; include marginal vx PDF with Lévy-stable fits
		- Plot 4: Displacement PDF evolution — marginal Δx PDF at multiple lag times for N=40 vortex system; overlay Gaussian and Lévy-stable fits to show non-Gaussianity
		- Plot 5: Residence time distributions — log-log P(τ) for each N with fitted power-law lines and β_trap annotations
		- Plot 6: Ergodicity breaking — EB parameter vs N for vortex system and vs β for Lévy walk; include EB vs Δt curves
		- Plot 7: Regime map — (EB, α) plane with vortex filament points (colored by N), Lévy walk points (colored by β); annotate theoretical Holtsmark prediction (α=1.5, EB=0) and normal diffusion (α=1.0, EB=0); if 2D superdiffusion_v2 results are available in the workspace load and overlay them, otherwise include a labeled placeholder region for the 2D Cauchy prediction
		- Save all plots as high-resolution PNG files

- Step 7:
	* sub-task: Statistical validation via bootstrapping and KS tests
	* agent in charge: engineer

	* instructions:
		- Load all saved results from Steps 1–5
		- Perform bootstrapping (500 resamples at tracer level) for each N to generate 95% confidence intervals for α(N) and EB(N); print bootstrap distribution widths
		- Conduct KS tests comparing empirical speed distributions against theoretical Holtsmark (scipy.stats.levy_stable with α_stable=1.5, β_skew=0); first estimate scale and location parameters from the empirical data and use those fitted parameters in the KS test; print fitted scale and location parameters alongside KS statistics and p-values for each N
		- Conduct KS tests comparing empirical displacement PDFs at multiple lag times against Gaussian null hypothesis for each N; print KS statistics and p-values
		- Print all results in a detailed summary table
		- Save all validation results to disk

- Step 8:
	* sub-task: Write the full Results section of the paper
	* agent in charge: researcher

	* instructions:
		- Synthesize all quantitative results from Steps 1–7 into a comprehensive Results section
		- Report velocity variance σ_v²(N) and its scaling with N; discuss implications for disorder intensity
		- Report α(N) for all vortex configurations with bootstrap confidence intervals; compare against the theoretical Holtsmark prediction α=1.5 and the 2D Cauchy prediction α→1; discuss convergence with increasing N
		- Report VACF zero-crossing times τ_c(N) and compare against Lévy walk VACF profiles; discuss the role of quenched spatial disorder vs stochastic renewal in generating velocity memory
		- Report Hill estimates of α_stable and tail exponent fits for the velocity PDF; assess convergence toward the Holtsmark distribution
		- Report displacement PDF non-Gaussianity and its evolution with lag time; discuss the Lévy-stable fitted parameters
		- Report residence time tail exponents β_trap(N) and their connection to the anomalous diffusion exponent
		- Report EB(N) values with bootstrap confidence intervals; compare against Lévy walk EB values; discuss ergodicity breaking as a function of filament density
		- Explicitly quantify and discuss the finite-sample effect from only 5 tracers per N configuration as a dataset limitation
		- Report regime map positions of all systems in the (EB, α) plane; explicitly contrast 3D vortex filament results with 2D point-vortex results
		- Reference all generated plots by their descriptive names
		- Provide a coherent physical interpretation connecting all results to the Holtsmark distribution theory and the 3D Biot-Savart kernel

name: plan_recorder
content: Plan recorded as final. Planning stage complete.
